title: Create FastAPI app entry point with health endpoint
description:
Create app/main.py (the FastAPI application) and app/api/ route module stubs, plus the health check endpoint.

WHAT TO DO:
1. Create app/main.py with:
   - FastAPI app instance with title, description, version
   - Router includes for rooms, bookings, availability (all prefixed /api/v1)
   - GLOBAL EXCEPTION HANDLERS for HomestayError subclasses:
     @app.exception_handler(RoomNotFoundError) → 404
     @app.exception_handler(BookingNotFoundError) → 404
     @app.exception_handler(RoomNotAvailableError) → 409
     @app.exception_handler(InvalidStatusTransitionError) → 400
     @app.exception_handler(BookingValidationError) → 400
     This eliminates try/except from individual route handlers (Fix I15).
   - Health check endpoint (Fix I10):
     GET /api/v1/health → executes 'SELECT 1' against the DB
     Returns {success: true, data: {status: 'healthy'}} on success
     Returns 503 {success: false, error: {code: 'DATABASE_UNAVAILABLE'}} on failure

2. Create placeholder route files:
   - app/api/__init__.py (empty)
   - app/api/rooms.py (empty APIRouter)
   - app/api/bookings.py (empty APIRouter)
   - app/api/availability.py (empty APIRouter)

REVIEW FIXES APPLIED:
- [I10] Health check verifies database connectivity
- [I15] Global exception handlers for typed exceptions

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- GET /api/v1/health returns 200 when DB is up, 503 when DB is down
- Global exception handlers map HomestayError subclasses to HTTP status codes
- OpenAPI docs accessible at /docs
close_reason: Completed: FastAPI app with global exception handlers, health check, and API routers

title: Verify full integration: API server, MCP server, and all tests
description:
Run the complete test suite and verify both the REST API and MCP server start correctly.

WHAT TO DO:
1. Run 'uv run pytest -v --tb=short' — all ~42 tests must pass
2. Start the REST API: 'uv run uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000'
   - Verify: curl http://localhost:8000/api/v1/health → success
   - Verify: curl http://localhost:8000/docs → OpenAPI UI loads
   - Stop the server
3. Verify MCP server starts: echo MCP initialize JSON | uv run python -m mcp_server.server
   - Should return MCP initialization response with server capabilities

WHY THIS STEP: Unit tests use SQLite. This integration step verifies everything works against real PostgreSQL and that both entry points (REST and MCP) start without import errors or configuration issues. It catches environment-specific problems that unit tests miss.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- All tests pass
- REST API serves requests on port 8000
- MCP server responds to initialization
- OpenAPI docs are accessible at /docs

comments.author: claude-opus
comments.text: INTEGRATION CHECKPOINT: This is the 'smoke test' against real infrastructure. Even though all unit tests pass with SQLite, this step catches: (1) Postgres-specific SQL issues (JSONB, FOR UPDATE), (2) migration problems, (3) import errors in entry points, (4) environment configuration issues. It's a 5-minute manual verification that prevents embarrassing failures in production.

title: Homestay Management System MVP
description:
Top-level epic for the entire Homestay Management System MVP. This system manages room inventory, availability checking, and booking/reservation lifecycle for small accommodation properties (homestays). It exposes both a REST API (FastAPI with auto-generated OpenAPI 3.1 spec) and an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server, enabling both traditional HTTP clients and AI agents/assistants to interact with it.

BUSINESS CONTEXT: Homestays are small-scale accommodations (1-15 rooms) typically run by individual hosts. Unlike full hotels, they lack dedicated front-desk staff, complex POS systems, or large operational teams. This system focuses on the core operational needs: knowing which rooms exist, which are available on which dates, and managing the booking lifecycle from creation through check-out.

TECHNICAL APPROACH: Python 3.13 + FastAPI + SQLAlchemy async + PostgreSQL 18 + Alembic + FastMCP 3.x. Layered architecture where both REST and MCP interfaces share a common service layer, ensuring consistent business logic regardless of access method.

MVP SCOPE (deliberately minimal): Rooms CRUD, date-range availability queries, booking lifecycle (create/confirm/cancel/check-in/check-out). No auth, no payments, no separate guest table, no channel management, no reporting. These are all planned for future iterations.

AI-AGENT FRIENDLINESS: Consistent JSON response envelopes, semantic error codes (not just HTTP status codes), idempotency keys on mutations, OpenAPI 3.1 auto-generated spec, MCP tools with rich descriptions. See design doc at docs/plans/2026-03-01-homestay-management-design.md.

comments.author: claude-opus
comments.text: DESIGN DOC: docs/plans/2026-03-01-homestay-management-design.md. IMPL PLAN: docs/plans/2026-03-01-homestay-implementation-plan.md. Both committed to git. The design doc was collaboratively developed with the user through a brainstorming session covering: scale (flexible 1-15 rooms), tech stack (Python/FastAPI chosen for auto-OpenAPI), database (Postgres chosen for transactional integrity), MVP scope (rooms + availability + bookings only), auth (none for MVP), and MCP support (yes, dual REST+MCP interface).

title: Create test harness with pytest fixtures and SQLite
description:
Create tests/__init__.py and tests/conftest.py with pytest fixtures for async database sessions and FastAPI test client.

WHAT TO DO:
1. Create tests/__init__.py (empty)
2. Create tests/conftest.py with:
   - SQLite async engine (sqlite+aiosqlite:///./test.db)
   - setup_db fixture: creates all tables before each test, drops after (autouse)
   - session fixture: yields an AsyncSession for direct service-layer testing
   - client fixture: yields an httpx.AsyncClient configured with ASGITransport for API testing

KEY DESIGN DECISIONS:
- Tests use SQLite, not Postgres. This means tests run without any external dependencies and are fast. The trade-off: SQLite doesn't support FOR UPDATE locks or JSONB, so some Postgres-specific features can't be tested in unit tests. Integration tests (Task 13) verify against real Postgres.
- The client fixture overrides FastAPI's get_session dependency to inject the test session. This ensures API tests use the same session (and thus the same in-memory state) as the test.
- setup_db is autouse=True so every test gets a clean database. No test pollution.

IMPORTANT: This fixture file imports from app.database (Base, get_session) and app.main (app), which don't exist yet. That's OK — these fixtures won't be called until those modules exist. The import structure is forward-looking.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- tests/conftest.py exists with setup_db, session, and client fixtures
- File is syntactically valid Python
- Fixtures use pytest-asyncio compatible patterns
close_reason: Completed: pytest fixtures with SQLite, setup_db autouse, session, and client fixtures

comments.author: claude-opus
comments.text: TEST STRATEGY: Unit tests use SQLite (fast, zero-config). Integration tests (bd-178) verify against real Postgres. The conftest.py creates/drops ALL tables before/after each test (autouse fixture), preventing test pollution. The client fixture overrides FastAPI's dependency injection to use the test session. This pattern is from the official FastAPI testing docs.

title: Implement RoomService with CRUD and availability generation
description:
Create app/services/__init__.py and app/services/room_service.py with all room management operations.

WHAT TO DO:
1. Create app/services/__init__.py (empty)
2. Implement RoomService class that takes an AsyncSession in its constructor.

METHODS:
- create_room(data: RoomCreate) -> Room
  • Creates the Room ORM object from schema data
  • Flushes to get the room.id
  • Generates availability_window_days (default 365) RoomAvailability rows
  • NOTE: RoomAvailability now uses composite PK (room_id, date) — no UUID id
  • Commits and returns the room
  
- list_rooms(room_type?, status?, page, per_page) -> tuple[list[Room], int]
  • FIX I9: When no status filter is provided, DEFAULT to excluding inactive rooms:
    if not status: query = query.where(Room.status != 'inactive')
    This prevents soft-deleted rooms from appearing in normal listings. Callers can still explicitly request status='inactive' to see them.
  • FIX N6: MUST include .order_by(Room.room_number) for consistent pagination.
  
- get_room(room_id: UUID) -> Room
  Raise RoomNotFoundError (from app.exceptions) if not found, instead of returning None. This makes the caller code cleaner — no None checks needed.
  
- update_room(room_id: UUID, data: RoomUpdate) -> Room
  Gets room (raises RoomNotFoundError if missing), applies non-None fields, commits.
  
- delete_room(room_id: UUID) -> Room
  SOFT DELETE: sets status='inactive'. Raises RoomNotFoundError if missing.

REVIEW FIXES APPLIED:
- [I9] list_rooms excludes inactive rooms by default
- [I15] get_room raises RoomNotFoundError instead of returning None
- [N6] Consistent ordering on list queries (ORDER BY room_number)

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- create_room generates availability rows using composite PK model
- list_rooms excludes inactive rooms when no status filter given
- list_rooms results are ordered by room_number
- get_room raises RoomNotFoundError instead of returning None
- delete_room sets status to 'inactive' (not hard delete)
close_reason: RoomService with CRUD, availability generation, inactive filtering, and typed exceptions

comments.author: claude-opus
comments.text: AVAILABILITY GENERATION: When a room is created, we generate 365 rows in room_availability with is_available=True. This is a batch insert (~365 rows). We use session.add_all() for efficiency. The 365-day window is configurable via settings.availability_window_days. A future task could add a scheduled job to extend the window as days pass, but for MVP this isn't needed — rooms just won't appear in availability queries beyond their window.

title: Write API tests for availability endpoints
description:
Create tests/test_api_availability.py with 2 tests for availability endpoints.

WHAT TO DO:
1. test_check_availability — create room, query availability for future dates, verify room appears with total_price calculated correctly
2. test_room_availability_calendar — create room, query 7-day calendar, verify 7 days all available

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:

- Total price calculation verified (3 nights * 75 = 225)
close_reason: 2 availability API tests pass: check_availability with total_price calculation, room_calendar 7-day range

title: Implement AvailabilityService with date-range queries
description:
Create app/services/availability_service.py with availability checking logic.

WHAT TO DO:
Implement AvailabilityService class that takes an AsyncSession.

METHODS:
- check_availability(check_in: date, check_out: date, guests: int) -> list[dict]
  • THE CORE QUERY: Find rooms where ALL dates in [check_in, check_out) are available AND max_occupancy >= guests AND status = 'active'
  • Implementation approach:
    1. Calculate dates_needed = [check_in + i days for i in range(num_nights)]
    2. Subquery: SELECT room_id FROM room_availability WHERE date IN dates_needed AND is_available=True GROUP BY room_id HAVING COUNT = num_nights
    3. Main query: SELECT rooms WHERE id IN subquery AND max_occupancy >= guests AND status = 'active'
  • Returns list of dicts with room info + total_price (nights * base_price)

- get_room_calendar(room_id: UUID, start_date: date, end_date: date) -> list[RoomAvailability]
  • Simple query: SELECT from room_availability WHERE room_id=X AND date BETWEEN start AND end ORDER BY date
  • Returns raw availability rows for a specific room's calendar view

QUERY DESIGN NOTE:
The check_availability query uses a subquery pattern:
1. Inner query finds room_ids that have available rows for ALL requested dates
2. HAVING COUNT = num_nights ensures ALL dates are available (not just some)
3. Outer query joins with rooms table to filter by capacity and status

This is efficient because room_availability has a composite unique constraint on (room_id, date), which Postgres will use as an index.

EDGE CASES:
- check_out <= check_in: return empty list (0 nights)
- Room has fewer availability rows than requested dates (e.g., dates beyond 365-day window): room won't appear (correct behavior — we can't guarantee availability for dates we haven't generated)

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- check_availability correctly finds rooms where ALL dates are free
- Filters by guest capacity
- Only returns 'active' rooms
- get_room_calendar returns ordered availability data
close_reason: AvailabilityService with check_availability (ALL dates must be free) and get_room_calendar

WHAT TO DO:

POST /api/v1/rooms (201 Created)

- Returns SuccessResponse[RoomResponse]

GET /api/v1/rooms (200 OK)

GET /api/v1/rooms/{room_id} (200 OK or 404)
- RoomNotFoundError auto-maps to 404 via global handler

PATCH /api/v1/rooms/{room_id} (200 OK or 404)
- FIX N3: Changed from PUT to PATCH (partial updates = PATCH semantics)
- Accepts RoomUpdate body (all fields optional)

DELETE /api/v1/rooms/{room_id} (200 OK or 404)
- Soft-deletes (sets inactive)
- Returns SuccessResponse[RoomResponse] with updated status

REVIEW FIXES APPLIED:

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:

- No try/except blocks in route handlers

title: Business Logic & API Layer
description:
Sub-epic covering Pydantic schemas, the service layer (room/availability/booking services), and all REST API endpoints. This is where the actual value lives — the business rules, validation, and HTTP interface.

WHY THIS GROUPING: The service layer is the heart of the system. Schemas define the contract. API routes are thin wrappers around services. These are developed in a TDD cycle: write schemas first (they define the interface), then service tests, then service implementation, then API tests, then API routes.

INCLUDES: Pydantic schemas (common envelope, room, booking, availability), RoomService, AvailabilityService, BookingService, FastAPI app entry point, health endpoint, rooms/availability/bookings API route handlers.

title: Create Pydantic schemas for availability queries
description:
Create app/schemas/availability.py with AvailabilityQuery, RoomAvailabilityDay, and AvailableRoom.

WHAT TO DO:

AvailabilityQuery:
- check_in: date
- check_out: date
- guests: int, default=1, gt=0

RoomAvailabilityDay (single day in a room's calendar):
- date: date
- is_available: bool
- booking_id: uuid.UUID | None
- model_config = {'from_attributes': True}

AvailableRoom (room returned from availability search):
- id, room_number, room_type, name, max_occupancy, base_price_per_night, amenities
- total_price: float — calculated field: num_nights * base_price_per_night
- model_config = {'from_attributes': True}

WHY AvailableRoom vs RoomResponse: AvailableRoom includes total_price (contextual to the search query) and excludes fields irrelevant to availability (description, status, timestamps). It's a projection, not the full room entity. This keeps API responses focused and reduces payload size.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- AvailableRoom has total_price field
- RoomAvailabilityDay has from_attributes enabled
- All schemas importable
close_reason: Completed: AvailabilityQuery, RoomAvailabilityDay, AvailableRoom schemas

title: Create Pydantic schemas for bookings (create, update, response)
description:
Create app/schemas/booking.py with BookingCreate, BookingUpdate, and BookingResponse.

WHAT TO DO:
Create three Pydantic models:

BookingCreate (POST /bookings request body):
- room_id: uuid.UUID, required
- guest_name: str, max_length=200, required
- guest_phone: str, max_length=50, required (user requirement: phone is mandatory)
- check_in_date: date, required
- check_out_date: date, required
- num_guests: int, gt=0, required
- special_requests: str | None, optional
- idempotency_key: str | None, max_length=100, optional — for safe retries

BookingUpdate (PATCH /bookings/{id} — limited fields, note PATCH not PUT):
- guest_name, guest_phone, special_requests, num_guests: all optional
- NOTE: dates cannot be changed via update (would require re-validating availability). Date changes require cancel + rebook. This is documented as a known MVP limitation.

BookingResponse (response body):
- All Booking fields including id, total_amount (as Decimal/float), status, timestamps
- room_number: str — ADDED (Fix N4). Populated from the eagerly-loaded room relationship. Much more useful than a raw UUID for humans and AI agents.
- cancelled_at: datetime | None — ADDED (Fix I7)
- cancellation_reason: str | None — ADDED (Fix I7)
- model_config = {'from_attributes': True}

CancelRequest (POST /bookings/{id}/cancel request body, optional):
- reason: str | None — why the booking is being cancelled

REVIEW FIXES APPLIED:
- [I7] Added cancellation_reason and cancelled_at to BookingResponse
- [N3] Documented as PATCH, not PUT, for partial updates
- [N4] Added room_number to BookingResponse for better UX

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- BookingCreate requires guest_phone
- BookingCreate does NOT have a total_amount field
- BookingResponse includes room_number, cancelled_at, cancellation_reason
- BookingUpdate cannot change dates or room_id
close_reason: Completed: BookingCreate, BookingUpdate, BookingResponse, CancelRequest schemas with room_number and cancellation fields

title: MCP Server & Integration
description:
Sub-epic covering the MCP server implementation, Docker Compose for local dev, and final integration testing. The MCP server is a key differentiator of this project — it allows AI assistants like Claude to directly manage the homestay without going through HTTP.

WHY THIS GROUPING: The MCP server is a parallel interface to the REST API. It depends on the service layer being complete but is otherwise independent of the API routes. Docker Compose and final integration are the 'ship it' tasks.

INCLUDES: FastMCP server with 10 tools, Docker Compose for PostgreSQL, .env.example, full test suite verification, initial push to remote.

title: Write API tests for rooms endpoints

Create tests/test_api_rooms.py with 7 tests covering all room endpoints (was 6, added 1).

Tests:
1. test_create_room — POST, assert 201, verify response shape
2. test_list_rooms — create room then GET list, verify data and meta.total
3. test_get_room — create then GET by ID, verify fields
4. test_get_room_not_found — GET with random UUID, assert 404 + ROOM_NOT_FOUND code
5. test_update_room — create then PATCH (was PUT) with partial update, verify change (Fix N3)
6. test_delete_room — create then DELETE, verify status='inactive'
7. test_list_rooms_hides_inactive — create room, delete it, list rooms → 0 results (Fix I9)

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- 7 tests pass (was 6)
- PATCH used instead of PUT for update
- Inactive rooms hidden from default list

title: Implement MCP server with FastMCP and all 10 tools
description:
Create mcp_server/__init__.py and mcp_server/server.py with all homestay management tools exposed via MCP protocol.

WHAT TO DO:
1. Create mcp_server/__init__.py (empty)
2. Create mcp_server/server.py with FastMCP 3.x server

SESSION MANAGEMENT (Fix C4):
DO NOT use 'async for session in _get_session()' pattern — it's an async generator misuse that can leak sessions on errors. Instead use direct context manager:
  async with SessionLocal() as session:
      service = RoomService(session)
      ...
Each tool function creates and cleanly closes its own session.

12 TOOLS TO IMPLEMENT (was 10, added 2 from Fix I14):

ROOM MANAGEMENT:
- list_rooms(room_type?, status?) → dict
- get_room(room_id: str) → dict
- create_room(room_number, room_type, name, max_occupancy, base_price_per_night, description?, amenities?) → dict  [NEW - Fix I14]
- update_room(room_id, room_type?, name?, max_occupancy?, base_price_per_night?, status?) → dict  [NEW - Fix I14]

AVAILABILITY:
- check_availability(check_in: str, check_out: str, guests: int=1) → dict

BOOKING LIFECYCLE:
- create_booking(room_id, guest_name, guest_phone, check_in_date, check_out_date, num_guests, special_requests?, idempotency_key?) → dict
- get_booking(booking_id: str) → dict
- list_bookings(status?, room_id?, check_in_from?, check_in_to?) → dict
- confirm_booking(booking_id: str) → dict
- cancel_booking(booking_id: str, reason?: str) → dict  [UPDATED: accepts reason]
- check_in(booking_id: str) → dict
- check_out(booking_id: str) → dict

ERROR HANDLING:
Tools catch HomestayError subclasses and return structured error dicts:
  except HomestayError as e:
      return {'error': e.code, 'message': e.message, 'details': e.details}
This gives AI agents machine-readable error information.

REVIEW FIXES APPLIED:
- [C4] Fixed session management — async with instead of async for generator
- [I14] Added create_room and update_room tools for full agent autonomy
- [I8] list_bookings supports date range filters
- cancel_booking accepts reason parameter

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- 12 tools registered (was 10)
- Each tool uses 'async with SessionLocal() as session:'
- Error returns include code, message, and details dict
- All tools have descriptive docstrings for AI agent consumption

comments.author: claude-opus
comments.text: AI-AGENT INTEGRATION: This is the key differentiator. The MCP server uses FastMCP 3.x (https://gofastmcp.com/) which is the most mature Python MCP SDK. Each tool has a detailed docstring that AI assistants (like Claude) read to understand when and how to use it. The tools accept strings for dates/UUIDs (not Python types) because MCP parameters come in as strings. Error returns are dicts, not exceptions, because agents handle structured data better than stack traces. The server runs as 'python -m mcp_server.server' with stdio transport.

title: Commit: REST API endpoints
description:
Stage and commit all API route files and tests.

WHAT TO DO:
1. Run 'uv run pytest -v' to verify all tests pass (~37 tests)
2. Stage and commit app/main.py, app/api/, tests/test_health.py, tests/test_api_rooms.py, tests/test_api_availability.py, tests/test_api_bookings.py

CHECKPOINT: After this commit, the REST API is fully functional. You can start the server with 'uv run uvicorn app.main:app' and interact with all endpoints. The OpenAPI spec at /docs is complete.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- All tests pass
- Clean commit

title: Create app config module with pydantic-settings
description:
Create app/__init__.py and app/config.py using pydantic-settings for environment-based configuration.

WHAT TO DO:
1. Create app/__init__.py (empty, makes it a Python package)
2. Create app/config.py with a Settings class that reads from environment variables with HOMESTAY_ prefix

SETTINGS NEEDED:
- database_url: PostgreSQL async connection string (default: postgresql+asyncpg://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/homestay)
- database_url_sync: PostgreSQL sync connection string for Alembic (default: postgresql+psycopg2://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/homestay)  
- availability_window_days: How many days of availability to pre-generate when a room is created (default: 365)

WHY PYDANTIC-SETTINGS: It reads from environment variables, .env files, and provides type validation. The HOMESTAY_ prefix avoids collision with other env vars. The Settings singleton pattern (module-level 'settings = Settings()') is idiomatic FastAPI.

WHY TWO DATABASE URLS: SQLAlchemy async uses asyncpg driver (postgresql+asyncpg://), but Alembic's migration runner uses synchronous psycopg2. Both point to the same database, just different drivers.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- app/config.py importable
- settings.database_url returns correct default
- Can override via HOMESTAY_DATABASE_URL env var
close_reason: Completed: Config module with pydantic-settings, env var override works

title: Initialize Alembic and create initial migration
description:
Initialize Alembic for async migrations, configure it to use our models and settings, generate and run the initial migration.

WHAT TO DO:
1. Run 'uv run alembic init alembic' to generate the alembic/ directory and alembic.ini
2. Edit alembic.ini: set sqlalchemy.url to empty (we'll set it from env.py)
3. Edit alembic/env.py:
   - Import asyncio, create_async_engine from sqlalchemy
   - Import settings from app.config
   - Import Base from app.database
   - Import all models (Room, Booking, RoomAvailability) so they register with Base.metadata
   - Set target_metadata = Base.metadata
   - Replace run_migrations_online() with async version using create_async_engine
4. Run 'uv run alembic revision --autogenerate -m "create rooms bookings and room_availability tables"'
5. Verify the generated migration file creates all 3 tables with correct columns
6. Create the PostgreSQL database: 'sudo -u postgres createdb homestay'
7. Run 'uv run alembic upgrade head'
8. Verify: 'sudo -u postgres psql -d homestay -c "\dt"' shows all tables

TECHNICAL NOTES:
- Alembic's default env.py uses synchronous engines. Since we use asyncpg (async driver), we need to modify env.py to use an async engine and run migrations inside an async context. This is well-documented in SQLAlchemy/Alembic docs but trips people up.
- The autogenerate feature compares Base.metadata against the current database state and generates migration operations. It handles CREATE TABLE, columns, indexes, and constraints.
- We clear sqlalchemy.url in alembic.ini and set it programmatically in env.py from our settings module. This keeps configuration in one place and supports env-var overrides.

PITFALL: If models aren't imported in env.py, autogenerate produces an empty migration. The 'from app.models import Room, Booking, RoomAvailability' line is critical.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- alembic.ini exists and is configured
- alembic/env.py uses async engine and imports all models
- alembic/versions/ contains the initial migration file
- Migration runs successfully against local PostgreSQL
- Tables exist in the homestay database
close_reason: Completed: Alembic initialized, async env.py, initial migration created and run successfully

title: Create SQLAlchemy async database engine and session factory
description:
Create app/database.py with the SQLAlchemy async engine, session factory, and declarative Base class.

WHAT TO DO:
1. Create app/database.py with:
   - create_async_engine() pointed at settings.database_url
   - async_sessionmaker (SessionLocal) configured with expire_on_commit=False
   - DeclarativeBase class (Base) — all ORM models inherit from this
   - get_session() async generator — FastAPI dependency that yields sessions

WHY expire_on_commit=False: In async SQLAlchemy, accessing attributes after commit triggers lazy loading, which requires a sync I/O call and fails in async context. Setting expire_on_commit=False keeps attributes accessible after commit without re-querying. This is the standard pattern for async FastAPI+SQLAlchemy.

WHY get_session AS A GENERATOR: FastAPI's Depends() system calls the generator, yields the session to the route handler, and ensures cleanup (session close) happens after the response is sent. This is the official FastAPI pattern for database session management.

ARCHITECTURE NOTE: This module is imported by everything — models, services, API routes, and the MCP server. It's the single point of database configuration. The test harness (conftest.py) overrides get_session to inject test sessions.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- app/database.py importable
- Base class usable as parent for ORM models
- get_session yields an AsyncSession
- Engine connects to the URL from settings
close_reason: Completed: Async engine, session factory, Base class, get_session generator

comments.author: claude-opus
comments.text: CRITICAL PATH: This module is imported by EVERYTHING — models, services, API routes, MCP server, and tests. The get_session async generator is the database session lifecycle manager. expire_on_commit=False is essential for async SQLAlchemy — without it, attribute access after commit triggers sync I/O which fails in async context.

title: Foundation & Data Layer
description:
Sub-epic covering all foundational work: project scaffolding, dependency management, database setup, SQLAlchemy ORM models, and Alembic migrations. This is the critical path — everything else depends on this layer being solid.

WHY THIS GROUPING: These tasks are tightly coupled. You can't write models without the DB engine, can't run migrations without models, and can't test anything without the test harness. They form a natural dependency chain that should be completed as a unit before any business logic work begins.

INCLUDES: pyproject.toml, uv setup, config module, SQLAlchemy Base/engine/session, Room/Booking/RoomAvailability ORM models, Alembic initialization and initial migration, PostgreSQL database creation, test fixtures (conftest.py with SQLite for tests).

title: Implement bookings REST API endpoints
description:
Replace app/api/bookings.py placeholder with full booking lifecycle endpoints. This is the largest API module.

WHAT TO DO:
Implement 8 endpoints. NOTE: Because app/main.py registers GLOBAL exception handlers for HomestayError subclasses, route handlers do NOT need try/except blocks. They simply call the service and let exceptions propagate to the global handler. This is much cleaner than the original plan.

POST /api/v1/bookings (201 Created)
- Accepts BookingCreate body
- Calls BookingService.create_booking()
- Exceptions auto-mapped: RoomNotAvailableError→409, PastDateError→400, OccupancyExceededError→400

GET /api/v1/bookings (200 OK)
- Query params: status?, room_id?, check_in_from? (date), check_in_to? (date), page=1, per_page=20
- FIX I8: Added date range filters so agents can ask 'who arrives tomorrow?'
- Returns ListResponse[BookingResponse]

GET /api/v1/bookings/{booking_id} (200 or 404)
- BookingNotFoundError auto-maps to 404

PATCH /api/v1/bookings/{booking_id} (200 or 404 or 400)
- FIX N3: Changed from PUT to PATCH (partial updates = PATCH semantics)
- Accepts BookingUpdate body

POST /api/v1/bookings/{booking_id}/confirm (200 or 400)
POST /api/v1/bookings/{booking_id}/cancel (200 or 400)
- Accepts optional CancelRequest body with reason
POST /api/v1/bookings/{booking_id}/check-in (200 or 400)
POST /api/v1/bookings/{booking_id}/check-out (200 or 400)
- All action endpoints: InvalidStatusTransitionError auto-maps to 400

REVIEW FIXES APPLIED:
- [I8] Date range filters (check_in_from, check_in_to) on list_bookings
- [I15] No try/except — global exception handlers do the mapping
- [N3] PUT→PATCH for partial updates

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- All 8 endpoints work
- No try/except blocks in route handlers (global handlers)
- PATCH used for partial update (not PUT)
- list_bookings supports date range filtering
- cancel accepts optional reason
close_reason: Completed: 8 booking endpoints with lifecycle actions

comments.author: claude-opus
comments.text: ERROR MAPPING PATTERN: The API route catches ValueError from the service layer and maps it to HTTP status codes: 409 for 'not available' (booking conflict), 400 for 'Cannot transition' (invalid state change), 400 for other validation errors, 404 for 'not found'. This keeps the service layer HTTP-agnostic (raises ValueError) while the API layer handles HTTP semantics. The same service can be used by the MCP server with different error handling.

title: Create RoomAvailability SQLAlchemy ORM model
description:
Create app/models/room_availability.py with the RoomAvailability ORM model.

WHAT TO DO:
Create app/models/room_availability.py with RoomAvailability class inheriting from Base.

COLUMNS:
- room_id: UUID, FK -> rooms.id, not null — PART OF COMPOSITE PRIMARY KEY
- date: DATE, not null — a specific calendar date — PART OF COMPOSITE PRIMARY KEY
- is_available: BOOLEAN, not null, default True
- booking_id: UUID, FK -> bookings.id, nullable — links to the booking that occupies this date

NO SEPARATE UUID 'id' COLUMN. Use (room_id, date) as the composite primary key:
  __table_args__ = (
      PrimaryKeyConstraint('room_id', 'date'),
      Index('ix_room_availability_date_available', 'date', 'is_available'),
  )

RELATIONSHIPS:
- room: many-to-one with Room
- booking: many-to-one with Booking (nullable)

REVIEW FIXES APPLIED:
- [I4] Removed wasteful UUID primary key. (room_id, date) is already unique and sufficient as a natural composite key. Saves 16 bytes per row. For 10 rooms * 365 days = 3,650 rows, that's ~58KB saved. Scales linearly.
- [I11] Added covering index on (date, is_available) for the primary availability query pattern: 'find rooms where all dates in range are available'. The composite PK already indexes (room_id, date), but the availability check groups BY room_id ACROSS dates, so an index starting with date helps.

WHY COMPOSITE PK WORKS WITH SQLALCHEMY:
  from sqlalchemy import PrimaryKeyConstraint
  class RoomAvailability(Base):
      __table_args__ = (PrimaryKeyConstraint('room_id', 'date'),)
      room_id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(UUID(as_uuid=True), ForeignKey('rooms.id'))
      date: Mapped[date] = mapped_column(Date)
      ...
SQLAlchemy handles composite PKs natively. The only change in service code: instead of session.get(RoomAvailability, some_uuid), use a query with WHERE room_id=X AND date=Y.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- RoomAvailability.__tablename__ == 'room_availability'
- Composite primary key on (room_id, date)
- NO 'id' UUID column
- Index on (date, is_available) exists
- booking_id is nullable
close_reason: RoomAvailability model created with composite PK and covering index

comments.author: claude-opus
comments.text: PERFORMANCE CONSIDERATION: For a 10-room homestay with 365 days of availability, this table has 3,650 rows. Even at 100 rooms, it's 36,500 rows — well within SQLite and Postgres comfort zones. The composite unique constraint (room_id, date) serves as a natural index for availability queries. If scale ever becomes an issue (unlikely for homestays), we could partition by month.

An agent checking room availability will call GET /api/v1/availability?check_in=2026-03-15&check_out=2026-03-18&guests=2 and get back a list of available rooms with prices. This is the 'search' before 'book' in the agent workflow.

title: Create Pydantic response envelope schemas
description:
Create app/schemas/__init__.py and app/schemas/common.py with the generic response envelope types.

WHAT TO DO:
1. Create app/schemas/__init__.py (empty)
2. Create app/schemas/common.py with:
   - Meta: pagination metadata (total, page, per_page)
   - ErrorDetail: error info (code, message, details dict)
   - SuccessResponse[T]: generic success envelope {success: true, data: T, meta: optional}
   - ErrorResponse: error envelope {success: false, error: ErrorDetail}
   - ListResponse[T]: list envelope {success: true, data: list[T], meta: Meta}

WHY A RESPONSE ENVELOPE:
AI agents need predictable response structures. Without an envelope, agents must guess whether a response is a single object, a list, or an error. The envelope pattern ensures:
- Every response has 'success: bool' at the top level
- Success responses have 'data' (the payload) and optional 'meta' (pagination)
- Error responses have 'error' with a machine-readable 'code' and human-readable 'message'
- Agents can parse errors without checking HTTP status codes (which some HTTP libraries hide)

WHY GENERIC TYPES: SuccessResponse[T] and ListResponse[T] use Python generics so FastAPI can generate correct OpenAPI schemas. When you return SuccessResponse[RoomResponse], the OpenAPI spec shows the exact shape of the data field.

DESIGN NOTE: ErrorDetail.details is a dict[str, Any] for flexibility. For ROOM_NOT_AVAILABLE errors, it can include next_available_date. For VALIDATION_ERROR, it can include field-level errors. The structure varies by error code.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- All schema classes importable from app.schemas.common
- Generic types work with arbitrary Pydantic models
- Error codes are strings, not enums (extensible without code changes)
close_reason: Created common.py with Meta, ErrorDetail, SuccessResponse, ErrorResponse, ListResponse

comments.author: claude-opus
comments.text: RESPONSE ENVELOPE RATIONALE: AI agents benefit from predictable response structures. Without an envelope, an agent must guess whether a 200 response contains a single object, a list, or an error. Our envelope ensures: (1) success/failure is always at the top level, (2) data is always in a 'data' field, (3) list responses always have pagination metadata, (4) errors always have machine-readable codes + human-readable messages. This is a pattern from Stripe, Twilio, and other 'API-first' companies.

title: Create custom exception classes for business logic errors
description:
Create app/exceptions.py with typed exception classes instead of using bare ValueError with string matching.

WHAT TO DO:
Create app/exceptions.py with:

class HomestayError(Exception):
    '''Base exception for all homestay business errors.'''
    def __init__(self, message: str, code: str, details: dict | None = None):
        self.message = message
        self.code = code
        self.details = details or {}
        super().__init__(message)

class RoomNotFoundError(HomestayError): ...
class BookingNotFoundError(HomestayError): ...
class RoomNotAvailableError(HomestayError): ...
class InvalidStatusTransitionError(HomestayError): ...
class BookingValidationError(HomestayError): ...
class PastDateError(BookingValidationError): ...
class OccupancyExceededError(BookingValidationError): ...

WHY THIS MATTERS (from design review):
The original plan used bare ValueError with string matching in the API layer:
  if 'not available' in error_msg: return 409
This is extremely fragile — if someone changes the error message wording, the HTTP status code changes. Typed exceptions let the API layer do clean pattern matching:
  except RoomNotAvailableError: return 409
  except InvalidStatusTransitionError: return 400

The API layer can also use FastAPI exception handlers registered globally, eliminating try/except blocks from individual route handlers.

ARCHITECTURE NOTE: These exceptions are part of the service layer contract. Services raise them, API routes and MCP tools catch them. The exception carries both a machine-readable code (for API responses) and a human-readable message.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- All exception classes defined with code and message
- HomestayError base class carries code, message, and details
- Importable from app.exceptions
close_reason: Created app/exceptions.py with HomestayError base and typed subclasses

title: Create Room SQLAlchemy ORM model
description:
Create app/models/room.py with the Room ORM model and app/models/__init__.py.

WHAT TO DO:
1. Create app/models/__init__.py importing all models (Room, Booking, RoomAvailability)
2. Create app/models/room.py with Room class inheriting from Base

COLUMNS (from design doc):
- id: UUID, primary key, default uuid4
- room_number: VARCHAR(20), unique, not null — the human-readable identifier (e.g., '101', 'A2')
- room_type: VARCHAR(50), not null — category like 'standard', 'deluxe', 'suite'
- name: VARCHAR(100), not null — display name like 'Garden View Room'
- description: TEXT, nullable — optional long description
- max_occupancy: INTEGER, not null — maximum number of guests
- base_price_per_night: DECIMAL(10,2), not null — default nightly rate. MUST use Mapped[Decimal] (from decimal module), NOT Mapped[float]. Float arithmetic causes rounding errors with money (e.g., 75.00 * 3 = 224.99999997).
- amenities: JSON, nullable — list of amenity strings like ['wifi', 'ac', 'parking']. MUST use sqlalchemy.JSON, NOT sqlalchemy.dialects.postgresql.JSONB. JSON works on both PostgreSQL (maps to jsonb) and SQLite (maps to TEXT with JSON serialization). JSONB would break the SQLite test suite.
- status: VARCHAR(20), not null, default 'active' — one of: active, maintenance, inactive
- created_at: TIMESTAMP WITH TZ, server_default=now()
- updated_at: TIMESTAMP WITH TZ, server_default=now(), onupdate=now()

RELATIONSHIPS:
- availability: one-to-many with RoomAvailability (cascade delete-orphan)
- bookings: one-to-many with Booking

REVIEW FIXES APPLIED:
- [C1] Changed JSONB → JSON for SQLite test compatibility
- [I12] Changed Mapped[float] → Mapped[Decimal] for money precision

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Room.__tablename__ == 'rooms'
- amenities column uses sqlalchemy.JSON (not JSONB)
- base_price_per_night uses Mapped[Decimal]
- All columns defined with correct types
close_reason: Room model created with correct column types (Decimal for money, JSON for amenities)

title: Initialize project with uv and pyproject.toml
description:
Run 'uv init --no-readme' and replace the generated pyproject.toml with our project config. Install all dependencies.

WHAT TO DO:
1. Run 'uv init --no-readme' in /tmp/homestay-management
2. Replace pyproject.toml with project-specific config including:
   - Core deps: fastapi, uvicorn[standard], sqlalchemy[asyncio], asyncpg, alembic, pydantic-settings, fastmcp>=3.0.0
   - Dev deps: pytest, pytest-asyncio, httpx, aiosqlite
3. Run 'uv sync --all-extras' to install everything and create .venv/
4. Verify: 'uv run python -c "import fastapi; print(fastapi.__version__)"' should succeed

WHY UV: uv is a fast Rust-based Python package manager that replaces pip+venv+poetry. It creates a lockfile (uv.lock) for reproducible installs and is 10-100x faster than pip. Already installed on this system.

WHY AIOSQLITE IN DEV: Tests use SQLite (via aiosqlite) instead of PostgreSQL for speed and zero-config. The async SQLAlchemy engine works with both asyncpg (Postgres) and aiosqlite (SQLite) — same ORM code, different driver. This is a standard pattern for FastAPI projects.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- pyproject.toml exists with all deps listed
- uv.lock exists (generated by uv sync)
- .venv/ exists with all packages installed
- 'uv run python -c "import fastapi, sqlalchemy, fastmcp"' succeeds
close_reason: Project initialized with uv, pyproject.toml configured with all dependencies, uv.lock generated

comments.author: claude-opus
comments.text: DEPENDENCY NOTE: This is the root task — everything else depends on it transitively. uv is already installed at /home/ubuntu/.local/bin/uv. Python 3.13.7 is available. We use uv instead of pip/poetry because it's faster and produces deterministic lockfiles. The --no-readme flag prevents generating a README.md (we'll create our own docs structure).

title: Create Booking SQLAlchemy ORM model
description:
Create app/models/booking.py with the Booking ORM model.

WHAT TO DO:
Create app/models/booking.py with Booking class inheriting from Base.

COLUMNS (from design doc + review fixes):
- id: UUID, primary key, default uuid4
- room_id: UUID, FK -> rooms.id, not null
- guest_name: VARCHAR(200), not null — full name of the primary guest
- guest_phone: VARCHAR(50), not null — mandatory contact phone (user requirement: phone is mandatory, email was dropped)
- check_in_date: DATE, not null — first night of the stay
- check_out_date: DATE, not null — the morning the guest leaves (exclusive)
- num_guests: INTEGER, not null — number of guests in the booking
- total_amount: DECIMAL(10,2), not null — MUST use Mapped[Decimal], NOT float. Calculated as num_nights * room.base_price_per_night using Decimal arithmetic.
- status: VARCHAR(20), not null, default 'pending' — state machine: pending -> confirmed -> checked_in -> checked_out, with cancel from pending/confirmed
- special_requests: TEXT, nullable — freeform guest notes
- idempotency_key: VARCHAR(100), unique, nullable — for safe booking retries by AI agents
- cancelled_at: TIMESTAMP WITH TZ, nullable — when the booking was cancelled (NEW from review)
- cancellation_reason: TEXT, nullable — why the booking was cancelled: 'guest_request', 'host_decision', 'no_show', or freeform text (NEW from review I7)
- created_at, updated_at: TIMESTAMP WITH TZ

RELATIONSHIPS:
- room: many-to-one with Room (use lazy='selectin' for eager loading of room_number)
- availability_dates: one-to-many with RoomAvailability

REVIEW FIXES APPLIED:
- [I7] Added cancelled_at and cancellation_reason columns for audit trail
- [I12] Changed total_amount to Mapped[Decimal]
- [N4] Added lazy='selectin' on room relationship so BookingResponse can include room_number without N+1 queries

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Booking.__tablename__ == 'bookings'
- total_amount uses Mapped[Decimal]
- cancelled_at and cancellation_reason columns exist
- FK to rooms.id
- idempotency_key is unique
close_reason: Booking model created with Decimal for money, cancellation audit fields

title: Implement BookingService with lifecycle and conflict prevention
description:
Create app/services/booking_service.py — the most complex service, handling booking lifecycle, availability locking, and state machine transitions.

WHAT TO DO:
Implement BookingService class that takes an AsyncSession.

STATE MACHINE:
VALID_TRANSITIONS = {
  'pending': ['confirmed', 'cancelled'],
  'confirmed': ['checked_in', 'cancelled'],
  'checked_in': ['checked_out'],
  'checked_out': [],
  'cancelled': [],
}

CUSTOM EXCEPTIONS (from app.exceptions):
All business errors MUST use typed exceptions, NOT bare ValueError:
- RoomNotFoundError — room doesn't exist
- RoomNotAvailableError — dates are booked
- InvalidStatusTransitionError — wrong state for this action
- PastDateError — check_in_date is in the past
- OccupancyExceededError — too many guests
- BookingNotFoundError — booking doesn't exist

METHODS:

create_booking(data: BookingCreate) -> Booking
  THE MOST CRITICAL OPERATION. Must be atomic and conflict-free:
  1. Check idempotency_key — if exists, return existing booking (safe retry)
  2. Validate check_in_date >= date.today() — REJECT PAST DATES (fix C3)
  3. Validate check_out > check_in
  4. Validate room exists (raise RoomNotFoundError if not)
  5. Validate num_guests <= max_occupancy (raise OccupancyExceededError if not)
  6. Calculate dates_needed
  7. Query room_availability rows — use .with_for_update() ONLY on PostgreSQL:
     query = select(RoomAvailability).where(...)
     if session.bind and session.bind.dialect.name != 'sqlite':
         query = query.with_for_update()
     This allows SQLite tests to exercise correctness logic while PostgreSQL gets real row locking. (Fix C2)
  8. If available_rows count != num_nights → raise RoomNotAvailableError with next_available_date in details
  9. Calculate total_amount using Decimal arithmetic: Decimal(str(room.base_price_per_night)) * num_nights (Fix I12)
  10. Create Booking with status='pending'
  11. Mark all availability rows: is_available=False, booking_id=booking.id
  12. Commit and return

get_booking(booking_id) -> Booking
  Raise BookingNotFoundError if not found.

list_bookings(status?, room_id?, check_in_from?, check_in_to?, page, per_page) -> tuple[list, int]
  ADDED date range filters (Fix I8): check_in_from and check_in_to filter bookings by check_in_date range. This enables 'who arrives tomorrow?' queries.
  MUST include ORDER BY created_at DESC (Fix N6) for consistent pagination.

update_booking(booking_id, data: BookingUpdate) -> Booking
  Cannot modify cancelled or checked_out bookings (raise InvalidStatusTransitionError).

_transition(booking_id, new_status) -> Booking
  Validates transition against VALID_TRANSITIONS.
  Raises InvalidStatusTransitionError (NOT ValueError) if invalid.

confirm_booking(booking_id) -> Booking — pending → confirmed
cancel_booking(booking_id, reason: str | None = None) -> Booking — pending/confirmed → cancelled
  • Sets cancelled_at = datetime.now(UTC) (Fix I7)
  • Sets cancellation_reason = reason
  • Releases availability (UPDATE room_availability SET is_available=True, booking_id=None WHERE booking_id=X)

check_in(booking_id) -> Booking — confirmed → checked_in
  • ADDED date validation (Fix I1): if date.today() < booking.check_in_date, raise InvalidStatusTransitionError('Cannot check in before check-in date')

check_out(booking_id) -> Booking — checked_in → checked_out
  • ADDED early checkout handling (Fix N11): on checkout, release any FUTURE availability dates still linked to this booking:
    UPDATE room_availability SET is_available=True, booking_id=None WHERE booking_id=X AND date > date.today()
    This allows early departures to free up remaining nights for new bookings.

REVIEW FIXES APPLIED:
- [C2] Conditional .with_for_update() — only on PostgreSQL dialect
- [C3] Past-date validation on booking creation
- [I1] Check-in date validation
- [I7] cancellation_reason and cancelled_at on cancel
- [I8] Date range filters on list_bookings
- [I12] Decimal arithmetic for money
- [I15] Custom exception classes instead of ValueError
- [N6] ORDER BY on list queries
- [N11] Early checkout releases future dates

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Booking creation rejects past dates (PastDateError)
- Check-in validates date (not before check_in_date)
- Early checkout releases future availability dates
- Cancel records reason and timestamp
- All errors use typed exceptions from app.exceptions
- list_bookings supports check_in_from/check_in_to filters
- FOR UPDATE is conditional on dialect
close_reason: BookingService with lifecycle state machine, typed exceptions, early checkout, cancellation audit

comments.author: claude-opus
comments.text: MOST COMPLEX SERVICE: This handles the booking lifecycle state machine and the critical booking-creation atomic operation. The SELECT...FOR UPDATE pattern prevents double-bookings in concurrent scenarios. In SQLite tests this is a no-op (acceptable because tests are single-threaded), but it's essential for production Postgres where multiple API requests may arrive simultaneously. The idempotency_key pattern is specifically designed for AI agent retries — an agent that gets a network timeout and retries the same booking won't create a duplicate.

title: Commit: project scaffolding, models, and migrations
description:
Create a .gitignore if needed, stage all foundation files, and commit.

WHAT TO DO:
1. Ensure .gitignore includes: __pycache__/, .venv/, *.pyc, .env, test.db, *.egg-info/
2. Stage: pyproject.toml, uv.lock, .python-version, app/__init__.py, app/config.py, app/database.py, app/models/, tests/__init__.py, tests/conftest.py, tests/test_models.py, alembic.ini, alembic/
3. Commit with descriptive message

THIS IS A CHECKPOINT: After this commit, we have a working foundation — models, migrations, test harness. All subsequent work builds on this. If anything goes wrong later, we can always reset to this point.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Clean git commit with all foundation files
- 'git status' shows clean working tree for tracked files
- 'uv run pytest tests/test_models.py -v' passes
close_reason: Foundation commit d53a856 with models, migrations, test harness
